issues.original: A physicist talks about a *force*. A data scientist talks about a *feature*. A game programmer talks about a *velocity*. A quantum theorist talks about a *state*. Different worlds, different languages — but the same underlying object: **a vector**.
issues.fixed: A physicist talks about a *force*. A data scientist talks about a *feature*. A game programmer talks about a *velocity*. A quantum theorist talks about a *state*. Different fields, different terms — but the same underlying object: **a vector**.

issues.original: That is not a coincidence. A vector is the smallest piece of mathematics flexible enough to describe **anything you can add together and scale**. Once you spot that pattern, you spot it everywhere.
issues.fixed: That's no coincidence. A vector is the simplest mathematical object flexible enough to describe **anything you can add together and scale**. Once you see this pattern, you'll see it everywhere.

issues.original: Most introductory courses give you two answers to 'what is a vector?':
issues.fixed: Most introductory courses provide two answers to 'what is a vector?':

issues.original: Both are correct. Both are also incomplete. The full story is that *a vector is whatever lives in a vector space* — a set of objects that play nicely with addition and scaling. Arrows and number lists are the two famous examples; functions, signals, polynomials and quantum states are equally valid.
issues.fixed: Both are correct but incomplete. The full story is that *a vector is whatever lives in a vector space* — a set of objects that work well with addition and scaling. Arrows and number lists are the most common examples; functions, signals, polynomials, and quantum states are equally valid.

issues.original: This chapter walks that ladder, from very concrete to genuinely abstract:
issues.fixed: This chapter moves from the concrete to the abstract:

issues.original:
### What You Will Learn
- How to think about vectors geometrically (arrows) and numerically (lists)
- The three operations: addition, scalar multiplication, subtraction
- The dot product — algebra and geometry agreeing on what 'alignment' means
- Several ways to measure size (norms), and why we need more than one
- The axiomatic vector-space definition — why functions can be vectors too
issues.fixed:
### What You Will Learn
- How to think about vectors geometrically (as arrows) and numerically (as lists)
- The three operations: addition, scalar multiplication, and subtraction
- The dot product — where algebra and geometry agree on what 'alignment' means
- Several ways to measure size (norms) and why we need more than one
- The axiomatic vector-space definition — why functions can also be vectors

issues.original: Stand at the centre of a park (call it the origin). A friend says: *'Walk 4 steps east, then 3 steps north.'*
issues.fixed: Stand at the center of a park (call it the origin). A friend says: *'Walk 4 steps east, then 3 steps north.'*

issues.original: That instruction **is** a vector. We write it as
issues.fixed: That instruction **is** a vector. We write it as

issues.original:
Two facts fall out immediately. The **magnitude** — how far you actually end up from the start — is just Pythagoras:
And the **direction** is the angle north of east:
So a vector packages two pieces of geometric information — *length* and *direction* — into a single object.
issues.fixed:
Two facts stand out. The **magnitude** — how far you end up from the start — is given by Pythagoras:
And the **direction** is the angle north of east:
So a vector combines *length* and *direction* into a single object.

issues.original: Here is the first idea that surprises beginners: **a vector does not care where it starts**. Whether you draw '4 east, 3 north' from the park centre or from the northeast corner, it is the *same* vector. Direction and length are the only invariants.
issues.fixed: Here's the first idea that surprises beginners: **a vector doesn't care where it starts**. Whether you draw '4 east, 3 north' from the park center or the northeast corner, it's the *same* vector. Direction and length are the only invariants.

issues.original: Velocity is the cleanest example. A ship sailing east at 20 knots has the same velocity vector whether it is in the middle of the Pacific or hugging the coast of Spain. The position changes from minute to minute; the velocity vector does not.
issues.fixed: Velocity is the clearest example. A ship sailing east at 20 knots has the same velocity vector whether it's in the middle of the Pacific or hugging the coast of Spain. The position changes every minute; the velocity vector does not.

issues.original: This is why, when we draw vectors, we are free to anchor them at the origin — a convenience, not a constraint.
issues.fixed: This is why, when drawing vectors, we can anchor them at the origin — a convenience, not a constraint.

issues.original: **Head-to-tail.** Walk along[FORMULA], then walk along[FORMULA]. The arrow from your starting point to your ending point is the sum. This is how we naturally think about successive displacements.
issues.fixed: **Head-to-tail.** Walk along [FORMULA], then walk along [FORMULA]. The arrow from your starting point to your ending point is the sum. This is how we naturally think about successive displacements.

issues.original: **Parallelogram.** Draw both vectors from the same point. Complete the parallelogram. The diagonal from that shared point is the sum. This is how physicists combine two forces acting at one location.
issues.fixed: **Parallelogram.** Draw both vectors from the same point. Complete the parallelogram. The diagonal from that shared point is the sum. This is how physicists combine two forces acting at the same location.

issues.original:
Drawing arrows works fine in two or three dimensions. The real power of the vector concept is that the *algebra* keeps working in **any number of dimensions**:
You cannot picture a 100-dimensional arrow, but every operation — addition, scaling, dot products, norms — carries over unchanged. This is the bridge from 'geometry you can see' to 'geometry you can only compute.'
issues.fixed:
Drawing arrows works fine in two or three dimensions. The real power of the vector concept is that the *algebra* works in **any number of dimensions**:
You can't picture a 100-dimensional arrow, but every operation — addition, scaling, dot products, norms — carries over unchanged. This bridges 'geometry you can see' to 'geometry you can only compute.'

issues.original: In most classrooms, determinants are introduced as a formula to memorize: You plug in numbers, compute, and move on. That misses the point entirely.
issues.fixed: In most classrooms, determinants are introduced as a formula to memorize: you plug in numbers, compute, and move on. This misses the point entirely.

issues.original: The determinant of [FORMULA] is the factor by which [FORMULA] scales area (in 2D) or volume (in 3D).
issues.fixed: The determinant of [FORMULA] is the factor by which [FORMULA] scales area (in 2D) or volume (in 3D).

issues.original: Once you internalize this, every property of determinants stops being a rule to memorize and starts being something you can *see*. The product rule [FORMULA] becomes obvious — two scalings compose multiplicatively. [FORMULA] means space gets crushed flat. [FORMULA] says the inverse must undo the scaling. The sign of the determinant tells you whether orientation was preserved or flipped.

issues.original: The unit square becomes a **parallelogram** spanned by those two columns. A short calculation — "outer rectangle minus the four corner triangles" — shows that the area of this parallelogram is That is the whole content of the 2D determinant.

issues.original: *Every* shape in the plane is rescaled by the same factor [FORMULA] — a circle of area [FORMULA] becomes an ellipse of area [FORMULA], a triangle of area [FORMULA] becomes a triangle of area [FORMULA], and so on. The matrix does not care about the shape, only about the local area element.

issues.original: Set the photocopier to "200%": Width doubles, height doubles, but **area quadruples** (not doubles). The determinant gives the area scaling directly, and that "[FORMULA]" is exactly the surprise built into linear maps.

issues.original: To build intuition, look at three different [FORMULA]'s acting on the unit square: - **Shear**, [FORMULA]: the parallelogram leans, but its area is unchanged. (Imagine pushing the top of a stack of books sideways — the volume of the stack does not change.) - **Stretch**, [FORMULA]: one direction is doubled; area doubles. - **Compression**, [FORMULA]: one direction is halved; area is halved.

issues.original: The absolute value [FORMULA] tells you about size. The **sign** tells you about *orientation*. - [FORMULA]: the transformation preserves orientation. A counter-clockwise loop stays counter-clockwise. - [FORMULA]: the transformation **flips** orientation. A counter-clockwise loop comes out clockwise — exactly what a mirror does.

A counter-clockwise loop stays counter-clockwise. - [FORMULA]: the transformation **flips** orientation. A counter-clockwise loop comes out clockwise — exactly what a mirror does.

issues.original: Take a right-hand glove. Rotate it, stretch it, squash it — it stays a right-hand glove. But turn it inside out, and it becomes a left-hand glove. That "inside-out" operation is exactly the kind of transformation a negative determinant performs in our model. Rotations and stretches keep [FORMULA]; reflections flip the sign.

That "inside-out" operation is exactly the kind of transformation a negative determinant performs in our model. Rotations and stretches keep [FORMULA]; reflections flip the sign.

issues.original: Ask any senior ML engineer "what math do you actually use day to day?" and the answer is almost always **linear algebra**. Calculus shows up in derivations; probability shows up in modeling; but the runtime of a real ML system is dominated by matrix-vector multiplies, decompositions, and projections. PyTorch's [CODE], scikit-learn's [CODE], Spark MLlib's [CODE], and a Transformer's attention head are all the same primitive in different costumes.
issues.fixed: Ask any senior ML engineer "what math do you actually use day to day?" and the answer is almost always **linear algebra**. Calculus appears in derivations; probability in modeling; but the runtime of a real ML system is dominated by matrix-vector multiplies, decompositions, and projections. PyTorch's [CODE], scikit-learn's [CODE], Spark MLlib's [CODE], and a Transformer's attention head are all the same primitive in different forms.

issues.original: This chapter walks through the algorithms that production ML systems actually run — PCA, LDA, SVM with kernels, matrix factorization for recommenders, regularized linear regression, neural network layers, attention — and shows the linear algebra that makes each of them tick. We focus on intuition first, geometry second, formulas third.
issues.fixed: This chapter covers the algorithms used in production ML systems — PCA, LDA, SVM with kernels, matrix factorization for recommenders, regularized linear regression, neural network layers, and attention — and explains the linear algebra behind each. We focus on intuition first, then geometry, and finally formulas.

issues.original: The reason is simple: **vectors are the atom of every linear algebra operation.** Once data is vectorized, you can take inner products (similarity), compute distances (k-NN), apply matrices (linear transforms), and decompose covariances (PCA). Without vectorization, you have to write a custom algorithm per data type; with it, the same SVD routine handles photos and protein sequences.
issues.fixed: The reason is simple: **vectors are the building blocks of every linear algebra operation.** Once data is vectorized, you can take inner products (for similarity), compute distances (for k-NN), apply matrices (for linear transforms), and decompose covariances (for PCA). Without vectorization, you need a custom algorithm for each data type; with it, the same SVD routine can handle photos and protein sequences.

issues.fixed: Caveat: real embeddings are 300 to 4096 dimensions, and the analogy property is statistical, not exact. The 2D picture above is a stylized illustration.
